Māori electorate in New Zealand

**Waiariki** is a New Zealand parliamentary [Māori electorate](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_electorates "Māori electorates") that was established for the [1999 election](/wiki/1999_New_Zealand_general_election "1999 New Zealand general election"), replacing the [Te Tai Rawhiti](/wiki/Te_Tai_Rawhiti "Te Tai Rawhiti") electorate. It is currently held by [Te Pāti Māori](/wiki/Te_P%C4%81ti_M%C4%81ori "Te Pāti Māori") co-leader [Rawiri Waititi](/wiki/Rawiri_Waititi "Rawiri Waititi"), who won it in the [2020](/wiki/2020_New_Zealand_general_election "2020 New Zealand general election") and [2023](/wiki/2023_New_Zealand_general_election "2023 New Zealand general election") general elections.

Waiariki was an important electorate in the 2020 election as Waititi's win allowed the Māori Party to re-enter parliament with two MPs, despite not reaching the 5% party vote threshold needed for parties without an electorate seat.

## Population centres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Waiariki_(electorate)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Population centres")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/City_of_Rotorua.jpg/250px-City_of_Rotorua.jpg)](/wiki/File:City_of_Rotorua.jpg)

[Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua")

The electorate includes the following population centres:

* [Tauranga](/wiki/Tauranga "Tauranga")
* [Whakatāne](/wiki/Whakat%C4%81ne "Whakatāne")
* [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua")
* [Taupō](/wiki/Taup%C5%8D "Taupō")

In the 2013/14 redistribution, a minor boundary adjustment was undertaken. A small area, including the village of [Tuai](/wiki/Tuai "Tuai"), was transferred to Waiariki from the [Ikaroa-Rāwhiti](/wiki/Ikaroa-R%C4%81whiti_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Ikaroa-Rāwhiti (New Zealand electorate)") electorate.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Waiariki_(electorate)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

The electorate was created for the [1999 election](/wiki/1999_New_Zealand_general_election "1999 New Zealand general election"). The first representative was [Mita Ririnui](/wiki/Mita_Ririnui "Mita Ririnui") of the [Labour Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_Labour_Party "New Zealand Labour Party"), with [Tuariki Delamere](/wiki/Tuariki_Delamere "Tuariki Delamere") (Te Tawharau) coming second, Arapeta Tahana ([Alliance](/wiki/Alliance_(New_Zealand_political_party) "Alliance (New Zealand political party)")) coming third and Kahukore Baker ([New Zealand First](/wiki/New_Zealand_First "New Zealand First")) coming fourth.[[3]](#cite_note-Electorate_Profile_Waiariki-3)

In the [2002 election](/wiki/2002_New_Zealand_general_election "2002 New Zealand general election"), Ririnui was confirmed with 61.93% of the electorate vote. Rihi Vercoe and Hamuera Mitchell of [Mana Māori](/wiki/Mana_M%C4%81ori_Movement "Mana Māori Movement") and the [National Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_National_Party "New Zealand National Party") came second and third, respectively.[[3]](#cite_note-Electorate_Profile_Waiariki-3)

In the [2005 election](/wiki/2005_New_Zealand_general_election "2005 New Zealand general election"), Ririnui was beaten by [Te Ururoa Flavell](/wiki/Te_Ururoa_Flavell "Te Ururoa Flavell") of the [Māori Party](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Party "Māori Party"). Hawea Vercoe of [Destiny New Zealand](/wiki/Destiny_New_Zealand "Destiny New Zealand") came a distant third.[[4]](#cite_note-Waiariki_2005_election-4) The [2008 election](/wiki/2008_New_Zealand_general_election "2008 New Zealand general election") was contested by two contenders: the incumbent and Ririnui. Flavell was once again confirmed.[[5]](#cite_note-Waiariki_2008_election-5)

The [2011 election](/wiki/2011_New_Zealand_general_election "2011 New Zealand general election") was contested by three contenders: Flavell, [Annette Sykes](/wiki/Annette_Sykes "Annette Sykes") of the [Mana Party](/wiki/Mana_Party_(New_Zealand) "Mana Party (New Zealand)") and Louis Te Kani of the Labour Party. Flavell had a comfortable lead over Sykes, with Te Kani coming third.[[6]](#cite_note-2011_election-6) In the [2014 election](/wiki/2014_New_Zealand_general_election "2014 New Zealand general election"), Flavell gained a much increased majority.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Labour's [Tāmati Coffey](/wiki/T%C4%81mati_Coffey "Tāmati Coffey") beat Flavell in [2017](/wiki/2017_New_Zealand_general_election "2017 New Zealand general election"). This left the Māori Party without any electorate seats, and consequently, no parliamentary representation as they had not reached the 5% party vote threshold required to enter Parliament without winning an electorate.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The Māori Party's [Rawiri Waititi](/wiki/Rawiri_Waititi "Rawiri Waititi") won the electorate back at the [2020 election](/wiki/2020_New_Zealand_general_election "2020 New Zealand general election"), which allowed the Māori Party to have two MPs.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
